column1: # Source: Z-Beam Laser Cleaning Research Lab
# Citation: Z-Beam (2025). Porphyry Laser Cleaning Dataset. Retrieved from http://localhost:3000/materials/stone/sedimentary/porphyry-laser-cleaning

column9: Optimal average power for Porphyry surface cleaning without thermal damage
Near-IR wavelength optimized for Porphyry mineral absorption characteristics
Beam spot diameter for precise cleaning of Porphyry surface features
Optimal repetition rate for efficient cleaning with thermal management
Fluence threshold for effective contaminant removal without substrate damage
Nanosecond pulse duration for controlled ablation of surface contaminants
Optimal scanning speed for uniform cleaning coverage
Recommended number of passes for complete contaminant removal
Optimal beam overlap for uniform cleaning without excessive thermal buildup

Typical range for industrial 1064 nm Nd:YAG lasers cleaning stone surfaces like porphyry; starting at 100 W for light contamination to avoid thermal damage given porphyry's low thermal conductivity (~2.5 W/mK); max safe below 500 W to prevent substrate cracking (e.g., IPG Photonics YLR series specs; academic review in J. Cult. Herit. 2018).

Effective for contamination removal on hard stones; 500 mm/s recommended for optimal balance of cleaning efficiency and heat dissipation; slower (100-500 mm/s) for heavy contamination, faster (1000-2000 mm/s) for light; based on industry standards from Adapt Laser Systems manuals.

Nanosecond regime preferred for cost-effective cleaning of porphyry; 20 ns minimizes heat-affected zone (HAZ) due to porphyry's high melting point (~1300°C) and reflectivity; picosecond (ps) or femtosecond (fs) for ultra-precise but less common industrially (Laser Phys. 2015 study on stone ablation).

Standard for 1064 nm cleaning; 30 kHz recommended for porphyry to achieve fast processing without excessive heat accumulation; higher rates (>50 kHz) speed up but risk thermal buildup on low-conductivity stones (Opt. Lasers Eng. 2019).

Fluence range for safe contaminant ablation on porphyry; 1.5 J/cm² recommended below damage threshold (~4 J/cm² for granite-like stones); conservative to account for porphyry's variable composition (Appl. Surf. Sci. 2017 ablation threshold study).

Beam diameter for industrial cleaning; 200 μm recommended for porphyry surface preparation, balancing fine detail and large-area coverage; smaller for precision, larger for broad contamination (Laserax equipment specifications).

Typically 1-2 passes for light industrial contamination on durable stones like porphyry; multi-pass for heavy buildup to ensure complete removal without exceeding safe fluence; from case studies in stone restoration (J. Laser Appl. 2020).

Ensures uniform coverage on irregular porphyry surfaces; 70% recommended for even cleaning without gaps or over-ablation; typical 50-80% in process optimization (Opt. Express 2016 study on scan strategies).
